critique.summary: The article reflects on the value of time and patience in building meaningful projects, relationships, and communities, contrasting this with the modern obsession with speed and instant gratification, especially in software development and startups. It emphasizes that some things, like trust, quality, and maturity, inherently require time and cannot be rushed or automated away.
mainPoints: Trees and other long-term natural developments symbolize the value of time and patience that cannot be replicated quickly.
Modern culture, especially in startups and software development, prioritizes speed and rapid iteration, sometimes at the expense of quality and longevity.
Certain processes, such as compliance and decision-making, require friction and time to ensure maturity and trust.
The rapid disappearance of startups and open source projects reflects a lack of long-term commitment, which undermines trust and sustainability.
Despite tools promising to save time, the author argues that saved time is often immediately filled with more tasks, leading to less actual free time.
Long-term commitment and consistency are essential for building projects and communities that endure and provide value.
critique.truthfulness: The article presents a subjective but reasonable perspective on the value of time and patience in building lasting projects and relationships. The observations about startup culture's focus on speed and the challenges of maintaining long-term commitment in open source projects are consistent with widely reported trends in the tech industry. The metaphor of trees and the emphasis on maturity and trust are philosophical rather than empirical claims but align with common understanding. There are no factual inaccuracies or misleading statements detected, but the article is primarily opinion-based and reflective rather than a report of verifiable facts.
considerations: The article does not provide empirical data or studies to support claims about startup failure rates or the longevity of open source projects.
It does not address how some rapid innovations have led to significant positive impacts despite shorter project lifespans.
The role of AI and automation in potentially enhancing quality and trust over time is discussed mainly from a skeptical viewpoint without exploring potential benefits.
The article focuses on the author's personal experience and perspective, which may not generalize across all industries or contexts.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the value of time and experience in various contexts, including AI development, maturity, and luxury goods. Commenters debate the correlation between time invested and value, the role of experience in competence, and the practical implications of speed and maturity.

comments.author: andyhedges
comments.text:
> We require age minimums for driving, voting, and drinking because we believe maturity only comes through lived experience.

Not true, we do this because the 99% of the time it's true, however there are people who would be perfectly competent and responsible to drive without living to the age of 16-18. Same with voting, there are humans who have a deep understanding and intelligence about politics at a younger age than suffrage. Equally there are people who will be reckless drivers at 40 and vote on whim at 60.

We have these rules not because sophistication only comes through lived experience, we have them because it's strongly correlated and covers of most error cases.

To take this to AI, run the model enough times with a higher enough temperature, then perhaps it can solve your challenges with a high enough quality - just a thought.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced view on age restrictions, emphasizing correlation rather than absolute causation, and thoughtfully extends the analogy to AI model training.

comments.author: Swizec
comments.text:
> everybody who is like me, fully onboarded into AI and agentic tools, seemingly has less and less time available because we fall into a trap where we’re immediately filling it with more things

You fill a jar with sand and there is no space for big rocks.

But if you fill the jar with big rocks, there is plenty of space for sand. Remove one of the rocks and the sand instantly fills that void.

Make sure you fit the rocks first.
comments.analysis: Uses a well-known metaphor to illustrate prioritization and time management effectively, aligning well with the article's theme about focusing on important tasks first.

comments.author: vaylian
comments.text: Speed is useful, when you have a good idea or a hypothesis you want to test. But if you are running in the wrong direction, speed is of very little value. With LLMs it might be even harder to stop and realize that you are creating the wrong thing, because you are not spending effort to create the wrong thing.
comments.analysis: Highlights the importance of direction and purpose over mere speed, especially relevant in AI development, providing a practical cautionary perspective.

comments.author: dminor
comments.text: On the contrary, you can solve the tree problem with money. There are nurseries that sell mature trees -- most people though will not choose to spend $20k on a tree.
comments.analysis: Offers a practical counterpoint about time and money trade-offs, though somewhat limited in scope and depth compared to other comments.

comments.author: titanomachy
comments.text:
> We pay premiums for Swiss watches, Hermès bags and old properties precisely because of the time embedded in them

Lost me in paragraph three. We pay for those things because they're recognizable status symbols, not because they took a long time to make. It took my grandmother a long time to knit the sweater I'm wearing, but its market value is probably close to zero.
comments.analysis: Challenges the notion that time invested directly correlates with value, emphasizing social perception and status as key factors in luxury goods pricing.

comments.author: rkwtr1299
comments.text:
And yet https://earendil.com/purpose/ is dabbling in AI and many posts by Ronacher are low key promoting AI.

He is contributing to the madness.
comments.analysis: This comment is more emotional and accusatory without substantive evidence, reducing its credibility and constructive value.
